metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `evidence_ref` in the plan: specify its type (e.g. string | null), what it references (a log line, a sidecar record ID, etc.), whether it is required or optional, and add an AC assertion (or explicit 'may be null/empty' note) so the field's contract is verifiable.
evidence: 'build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref) → {schema:"gate_error_v1", verdict:"ERROR", gate:<plan_review|code_review|completion>, error:{cause,evidence_ref}}'
AC1 checks '.verdict=="ERROR"' and 'non-empty error.cause' — evidence_ref has no coverage
findings.finding: The `evidence_ref` field is described in the payload shape (`build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref)` → `{…error:{cause,evidence_ref}}`) but is never defined — its type, allowed values, nullability, and what it references are unspecified — and no AC verifies it (AC1 checks only `non-empty error.cause`, leaving `evidence_ref` as an untested, underdefined field.
findings.location: Files & approach (build_error_payload signature) + AC1
scenarios: Implementer must guess the type/contract for evidence_ref; the metrics consumer (ticket 3c07) may receive a null or absent field it doesn't expect.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note such as: '`evidence_ref`: optional string — a reference to a related sidecar record ID or log anchor; may be null if unavailable. AC1 should assert `error.evidence_ref` is either a non-empty string or explicitly null.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `evidence_ref` in the plan: state its type (e.g. `str | None`), its content convention (e.g. the event/log ID of the triggering exception record, or `null` when unavailable), and add a corresponding assertion to AC1's jq command.
evidence: Plan defines the builder as `build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref)` but provides no definition of what `evidence_ref` is, what type it has (string path? event ID? None?), or when it may be null/empty.
No AC or test assertion references `evidence_ref` content, making it untestable as written.
findings.finding: `evidence_ref` is included in the `build_error_payload` signature and `error` object schema but is never defined — its type, semantics, or acceptable values are unspecified, forcing the executor to make a design decision.
findings.location: Files & approach section — `build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref)` and `{schema:"gate_error_v1", ..., error:{cause,evidence_ref}}`
scenarios: An executor implementing `build_error_payload` must invent the semantics of `evidence_ref`, potentially producing an incompatible schema with the intended metrics reader (ticket 3c07).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the schema definition: `evidence_ref: str | None — the event-store record ID of the gate invocation that raised the exception, or null if unavailable at emit time`. Update AC1 to assert `jq -e '.error.cause | length > 0'` and note that `evidence_ref` may be null.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `evidence_ref`'s type, semantics, and format (e.g. a nullable log-line string, a structured `{event_id, step_id}` dict, or a plain exception repr) and add it to the `gate_error_v1` schema spec and to the AC's `jq` assertion.
evidence: Plan text: 'a small dedicated payload builder `build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref)` → `{schema:"gate_error_v1", verdict:"ERROR", gate:<plan_review|code_review|completion>, error:{cause,evidence_ref}}`'
No definition of evidence_ref type (string? dict? log reference? URL?) appears anywhere in the plan.
The Acceptance Criteria assert only `non-empty error.cause` — evidence_ref validity is entirely untested.
Absence: no codebase precedent for a field named evidence_ref in any sidecar module was found.
findings.finding: The `evidence_ref` field is named in the `build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref)` signature and in the payload schema `error:{cause, evidence_ref}`, but its type, semantics, allowed values, and format are never defined anywhere in the plan — implementors must invent the contract for a named interface.
findings.location: Files & approach section: '`build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref)` → `{schema:"gate_error_v1", verdict:"ERROR", gate:<...>, error:{cause,evidence_ref}}`'
scenarios: Implementor writes evidence_ref as a free-form string; ticket-3c07 reader expects a structured dict — schema mismatch silently degrades the diagnosis interval.
Different gate sites (plan-review preflight vs mid-run vs completion) produce evidence_ref values of incompatible types, breaking the 3c07 consumer's ability to correlate records.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a schema subsection to the plan: `error.cause` = stringified exception class+message; `error.evidence_ref` = nullable string — the workflow step id or preflight log reference where the exception was caught (e.g. `"preflight"` or the recorder step-id), or `null` when no step context is available. Update the AC to assert `jq -e '.error.evidence_ref != null or .error.evidence_ref == null'` shape validity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `evidence_ref` in the `gate_error_v1` schema: specify its type (e.g., `str | None`), its semantics (e.g., 'the sanitized LLM diagnostic string from `LLMOutcome.diagnostic`, or None when the error carries no outcome'), and whether the AC should assert non-emptiness or allow null.
evidence: Plan text: 'build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref) → {schema:"gate_error_v1", verdict:"ERROR", gate:<plan_review|code_review|completion>, error:{cause,evidence_ref}}'
AC line: 'a persisted gate_error_v1 record with jq -e \'.\.verdict=="ERROR"\' and non-empty error.cause' — only cause is AC-asserted; evidence_ref has no AC assertion or definition
ABSENCE: no plan section defines the type (string? dict? optional?), semantics (what does it refer to — a log line, an event file path, an exception traceback?), or whether it may be null/empty
Search for 'evidence_ref' in src/rebar/llm returns zero matches — it does not exist in the codebase yet, so there is no implicit precedent to inherit
findings.finding: `evidence_ref` is named in the `build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref)` signature and in the payload schema `error:{cause, evidence_ref}` but is never defined — its type, allowed values, semantics, and nullability are entirely unspecified in the plan. An implementer cannot write the builder or the downstream reader without inventing these details.
findings.location: Files & approach section: '`build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref)` → `{schema:"gate_error_v1", verdict:"ERROR", gate:<plan_review|code_review|completion>, error:{cause,evidence_ref}}`'
scenarios: Implementer writes build_error_payload and must decide: is evidence_ref a string snippet from the exception traceback? An event file reference? An LLMOutcome.diagnostic dict? A None-able field? Different interpretations produce incompatible payloads.

Downstream consumer (ticket 3c07 metrics reader) reads gate_error_v1 and references error.evidence_ref — if the field shape differs from what the consumer expects (e.g., it expects a string but gets a dict), the consumer silently misreads every ERROR record.
Unit test in test_gate_error_sidecar.py must assert something about evidence_ref to be meaningful, but without a definition the test author also has to invent the semantics.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Files & approach' section, expand the payload description to: `error:{cause: str, evidence_ref: str | None}` where `cause` is `str(exc)` and `evidence_ref` is `outcome.diagnostic` (from `failure.outcome_of(exc)`) when available, else `None`. Update the AC to assert `error.evidence_ref` is either a non-empty string or explicitly null (not absent).

findings.finding: The `error` object schema includes an `evidence_ref` field that is never defined — its type, what it references (a log line, a span ID, a file path?), and whether it is optional or required are all unstated. An executor cannot implement this field without guessing.
findings.location: What section — '`error{cause,evidence_ref}` object'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '`evidence_ref`: optional string, the raw provider error message or HTTP response body excerpt (≤500 chars), or null if unavailable. Omit the key rather than write null to keep the payload clean.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC1 proving command to explicitly assert non-empty cause, e.g. `jq -e '.verdict=="ERROR" and (.error.cause | length > 0)'` so both sub-claims are machine-checked in a single expression.
evidence: AC1 reads: '…yields a persisted `gate_error_v1` record with `jq -e \'.verdict=="ERROR"'` and non-empty `error.cause`' — the second half is prose, not a command.
The defect taxonomy item (5) warns: missing cardinality/content assertion when the criterion names a value check.
findings.finding: AC1's jq proving command verifies `verdict=="ERROR"` but leaves the 'non-empty error.cause' assertion in prose only, with no concrete anchored jq expression — the command as written does not fully prove the criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet: `jq -e '.verdict=="ERROR"'` and non-empty `error.cause`
scenarios: A conforming implementation that writes `error: {cause: "", evidence_ref: null}` would pass the stated jq command but violate the criterion's intent.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC1 command with: `jq -e '.verdict=="ERROR" and (.error.cause | type == "string") and (.error.cause | length > 0)'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name both `_code_review_preflight` (catch at line 536) and `_run_code_review_gate` (catch at line 609) as distinct ERROR-sidecar emit sites for the code-review gate, so the implementation covers both sub-function exception paths.
evidence: Plan states: 'produce_code_review_verdict (:512) … catch LLMUnavailableError … emit the ERROR record, then return the existing degraded INDETERMINATE verdict'

Actual code: produce_code_review_verdict at line 512 is a 3-line orchestrator calling _code_review_preflight (LLMUnavailableError catch at line 536, returns _degraded_code_review_verdict) and _run_code_review_gate (LLMUnavailableError catch at line 609, also returns _degraded_code_review_verdict)
Both internal helpers catch independently — the plan names neither sub-function, so it is ambiguous which receives the new emit call
findings.finding: The plan conflates the code-review gate's TWO separate LLMUnavailableError catch sites into a single reference at ':512'. The function produce_code_review_verdict (:512) is a thin orchestrator that delegates to _code_review_preflight (catch at line 536) and _run_code_review_gate (catch at line 609). The ERROR sidecar emit must be added at BOTH sub-function sites independently; the plan's singular ':512' reference risks the implementer adding the emit at only one of them and silently missing the other.
findings.location: Files & approach section — 'produce_code_review_verdict (:512)'
scenarios: Implementer adds build_error_payload emit only to _code_review_preflight (the preflight path) and misses _run_code_review_gate (the mid-run outage path) — mid-run code-review outages go unrecorded.
Implementer adds emit only to _run_code_review_gate and misses the preflight site — preflight-level outages go unrecorded.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the singular ':512' reference with: 'code-review infra sites: _code_review_preflight (:536) for preflight failures, and _run_code_review_gate (:609) for mid-run failures — emit the ERROR record at each catch site, then return the existing degraded INDETERMINATE verdict.'

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan which module owns `build_error_payload` for the code-review ERROR path — either name `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` as an edit target or explicitly state that `gate_dispatch.py` imports the shared helper from `plan_review/sidecar.py` for all review-type REVIEW_RESULT emissions.
evidence: The plan lists only plan_review/sidecar.py and completion_sidecar.py as receiving build_error_payload, but code-review ERROR records must also be written (gate_dispatch.py:536 and :609 are the code-review LLMUnavailableError sites).
src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py exists as a separate module with its own EVENT_TYPE='REVIEW_RESULT' and emit path (confirmed by listing).
The plan says 'plan-review + code-review infra sites: emit the ERROR record' but does not clarify the import/module home for the code-review ERROR payload builder.
findings.finding: The plan names `plan_review/sidecar.py` and `completion_sidecar.py` as the homes for `build_error_payload`/`emit_error`, but the code-review ERROR path (gate_dispatch.py lines ~536 and ~609) also needs to emit a `gate_error_v1` REVIEW_RESULT record. The code-review sidecar (`src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`) is not named as an edit target, leaving it unclear whether `gate_dispatch.py` should import `build_error_payload` from `plan_review/sidecar.py` for both plan- and code-review errors, or whether `code_review/sidecar.py` also needs the helper.
findings.location: Files & approach section — 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py, src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py — add the build_error_payload helper'
scenarios: Implementer places build_error_payload only in plan_review/sidecar.py and imports it from gate_dispatch.py for both review types — works but creates a cross-module dependency not described in the plan.
Implementer creates a duplicate build_error_payload in code_review/sidecar.py — inconsistency with the plan's stated file list.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` to the edit targets list (or note it inherits from a shared location), and clarify the import chain so code-review ERROR emissions are unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the code-review ERROR sidecar emission policy when `target_ticket` is `None` — either skip silently (matching the existing 'sidecar only if target_ticket' convention already documented in the gate), or explain how the record is routed without a ticket context.
evidence: plan: 'plan-review + code-review infra sites: emit the ERROR record, then return the existing degraded INDETERMINATE verdict'
`CodeReviewRequest.target_ticket: str | None = None` — explicitly nullable
existing code-review sidecar already has a conditional: 'sidecar only if target_ticket' (gate_dispatch.py:515 docstring)
plan's `build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref)` signature has no ticket_id parameter — it is unclear how the emit would resolve a ticket dir when target_ticket is None
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens when `CodeReviewRequest.target_ticket` is `None` at the two code-review infra-exception catch sites. Without a `ticket_id`, the ERROR sidecar cannot be written to any ticket's event store, yet the plan asserts unconditional emission at these sites.
findings.location: Files & approach section (code-review infra sites); src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py:534-537 (_code_review_preflight), :609-610 (_run_code_review_gate); CodeReviewRequest.target_ticket type annotation line 495
scenarios: A local `rebar review-code` run (no target_ticket) hits an LLMUnavailableError at preflight — the plan says emit the ERROR record, but there is no ticket to write it to; the emit would fail or emit to the wrong place
A Gerrit voter run with a change_id but no target_ticket hits a mid-run infra failure — same issue
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'For code-review, the ERROR sidecar is emitted only when `request.target_ticket` is non-None — matching the existing convention for code-review sidecars; a ticketless run (local `review-code`, no session artifact) silently skips emission.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` to the Files & approach list and specify whether `build_error_payload` is a shared helper imported from `plan_review/sidecar.py` (analogous to how `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` is already shared) or duplicated with its own emit path.
evidence: Codebase confirms three sidecar modules: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`, `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`, `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py`.
Plan's file list omits `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` despite the code-review gate being one of the three gates receiving ERROR sidecar treatment.
Plan text: 'plan-review + code-review infra sites: emit the ERROR record' — but only plan_review/sidecar and completion_sidecar are listed in Files & approach.
findings.finding: The plan assigns `build_error_payload` to `plan_review/sidecar.py` and `completion_sidecar.py`, but the code-review infra path (which the plan says also emits ERROR records) uses a third, separate module `code_review/sidecar.py` — the plan does not specify whether the code-review error builder lives there, delegates to the plan-review helper, or is handled differently.
findings.location: Files & approach section: '`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`, `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` — add the `build_error_payload` helper + an `emit_error(...)` path'
scenarios: Implementor adds build_error_payload only to plan_review/sidecar.py and assumes code_review/sidecar.py will import it, but the import creates a coupling inconsistent with code_review/sidecar's current dependency structure.
Implementor duplicates the builder into code_review/sidecar.py, creating a second literal that drifts from the plan_review version.
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the existing `from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar import RETAIN_PER_TICKET` precedent: define `build_error_payload` and `emit_error` once in `plan_review/sidecar.py` and import them into `code_review/sidecar.py` and `completion_sidecar.py`. Update the Files & approach list to include `code_review/sidecar.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Drop `evidence_ref` from `gate_error_v1` / `build_error_payload` unless ticket 3c07 (or another named consumer in scope) explicitly reads it; if retained, add an AC that exercises it and documents what value is written at each call-site.
evidence: ACs reference only `jq -e '.verdict=="ERROR"'` and `non-empty error.cause` — no AC reads or validates evidence_ref.
The stated consumer ('The metrics gate-sidecar reader (ticket 3c07) is the consumer that reads gate_error_v1') does not mention evidence_ref in any acceptance criterion.
No other plan section describes what value is written to evidence_ref, who writes it, or how the consumer uses it — only cause is exercised in the unit-test assertion.
findings.finding: The `evidence_ref` field in the proposed `error{cause, evidence_ref}` object has no stated consumer in this ticket's ACs, done-definition, or in the named downstream consumer (ticket 3c07), making it a YAGNI addition.
findings.location: Files & approach section — '`build_error_payload(gate, cause, evidence_ref)` → `{schema:"gate_error_v1", verdict:"ERROR", gate:…, error:{cause,evidence_ref}}`'
scenarios: evidence_ref is silently written as null/empty on every real infra exception (nothing supplies it at the call-site), making it dead schema weight from day one.
If a future consumer does need a reference artifact, the field's semantics will be retroactively defined, risking incompatible interpretations of existing null records.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `evidence_ref` from the `build_error_payload` signature and the `gate_error_v1` schema. The three ACs are fully satisfied by `{schema, verdict, gate, error:{cause}}`. Reintroduce `evidence_ref` in the ticket that first consumes it, where its semantics can be pinned to a concrete use-case.

findings.finding: The `What` section describes the `error` object as having two fields — `cause` and `evidence_ref` — but neither AC nor the Tests section verifies `evidence_ref`, leaving it as an uncovered deliverable component with no acceptance criterion.
findings.location: What section (`error{cause,evidence_ref}`) vs Acceptance Criteria (only checks `error.cause`)
findings.suggested_fix: If evidence_ref is optional, update the What section to: `error{cause, evidence_ref?}` and add a note that it is populated when a relevant log ref exists. If it is required, add to AC1: 'and `.error.evidence_ref` is a string (may be empty)' and update the unit test accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the Tests section to explicitly describe a parallel code-review infra scenario (stubbing `produce_code_review_verdict` to raise `LLMUnavailableError`) that asserts both the `gate_error_v1` record emission and the INDETERMINATE return, matching the coverage AC2 requires.
evidence: Tests section: 'a stubbed plan-review run raising LLMUnavailableError writes a REVIEW_RESULT record … AND the gate still returns INDETERMINATE'
AC2: 'plan-review/code-review still return INDETERMINATE … asserted by the test'
No analogous code-review test scenario is described in the Tests section
findings.finding: The Tests section describes only a plan-review infra scenario to verify the INDETERMINATE-preservation contract, but AC2 explicitly requires both plan-review AND code-review to return INDETERMINATE — leaving the code-review test case described nowhere in the plan body, creating an implementation gap.
findings.location: Tests section + AC2
scenarios: An implementer following only the Tests section narrative writes only a plan-review test; AC2 then fails because the code-review degrade path is untested.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Tests section: 'Likewise, a stubbed code-review run raising LLMUnavailableError writes a REVIEW_RESULT record with schema="gate_error_v1" and the gate still returns INDETERMINATE.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md / design-doc entry capturing the `gate_error_v1` schema contract, the write-then-degrade / write-then-reraise pattern, and the named consumer (ticket 3c07), so the contract is discoverable and change-safe.
evidence: 'Dedicated schema tag `gate_error_v1`' — new schema contract introduced with no documentation update planned
'The metrics gate-sidecar reader (ticket 3c07) is the consumer that reads `gate_error_v1`' — cross-ticket dependency on an undocumented contract
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or schema-registry update in Files & approach or Back-compat sections
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new schema contract (`gate_error_v1`) and a new architectural pattern (ERROR gate-sidecar as env-diagnosis start marker) that a named downstream consumer (ticket 3c07 metrics reader) will depend on, but no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc update is mentioned to capture this contract.
findings.location: Files & approach section — '`gate_error_v1`' schema + 'The metrics gate-sidecar reader (ticket 3c07) is the consumer'
scenarios: A future author changes gate_error_v1 payload shape not knowing ticket 3c07 depends on it
New team members cannot discover the ERROR-record pattern or its diagnostic semantics from any design doc
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief section to AGENTS.md (or a new `docs/adr/gate_error_sidecar.md`) that specifies: the gate_error_v1 payload shape, the invariant that it is never routed through reconcile_verdict, the two write patterns (write-then-degrade, write-then-reraise), and the consumer dependency from ticket 3c07.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `gate_error_v1` writes are idempotent under retry (e.g., include a deduplication key such as a gate-run ID or timestamp), or document that the 3c07 consumer is expected to deduplicate by (gate, wall-clock window) before counting ERROR intervals.
evidence: 'completion infra site: wrap preflight (:651) so it emits the ERROR record then re-raises (write-then-reraise — the fail-closed contract is unchanged)'
'The metrics gate-sidecar reader (ticket 3c07) is the consumer that reads gate_error_v1'
No mention of deduplication key, idempotency token, or consumer-side duplicate handling in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `gate_error_v1` records written on the completion infra path (write-then-reraise) are idempotent under retry. If the gate caller retries after the caught-and-reraised `LLMUnavailableError`, a second ERROR record is appended for the same logical failure event. The downstream consumer (ticket 3c07 metrics reader) is not described as deduplication-aware, so duplicate ERROR records could cause double-counted diagnosis intervals or inflated error metrics.
findings.location: Files & approach — completion infra site: 'wrap preflight (:651) so it emits the ERROR record then re-raises'
scenarios: An agent retry loop retries a completion gate after a transient LLMUnavailableError; each attempt writes a fresh gate_error_v1 record before re-raising; the 3c07 reader counts N ERROR records for a single infra event, producing N diagnosis intervals instead of one.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `run_id` or `attempt_id` field to `build_error_payload` (sourced from the existing gate invocation context if available), and document that the 3c07 reader should group by `run_id` before counting diagnosis intervals.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `latest_review_timestamp` (sidecar.py:213, callers: attest.py:286, :338) as a consumer affected by gate_error_v1 REVIEW_RESULT records and document whether resetting its timestamp on an infra error is intentional or requires the function to also be schema-guarded.
evidence: latest_review_timestamp at sidecar.py:229-237 filters files by suffix `-REVIEW_RESULT.json` but applies NO schema check — it returns `int(prefix)` of the newest matching filename.
attest.py:286 and :338 call `sidecar.latest_review_timestamp` to gate remediation-mode eligibility by a freshness window.
The plan explicitly lists latest_review_result and latest_fail_verdict/latest_pass_record as covered by schema guards, but does not mention latest_review_timestamp.

An ERROR sidecar emitted mid-infra-failure for a ticket where a review was last run weeks ago would make latest_review_timestamp report 'now', potentially re-enabling a stale remediation-mode eligibility window.
findings.finding: `latest_review_timestamp` (plan_review/sidecar.py:213) is an unenumerated consumer that is affected by this change. It walks ALL `REVIEW_RESULT` files by filename with NO schema guard and returns the newest file's timestamp. Emitting a `gate_error_v1` REVIEW_RESULT record makes it the newest file, so `latest_review_timestamp` returns the error-emission time — resetting the remediation freshness window checked in attest.py (lines 286, 338). The plan only enumerates `latest_review_result` and `all_review_results` as schema-guarded readers that safely skip `gate_error_v1`, but omits `latest_review_timestamp`.
findings.location: Back-compat section — 'existing verdict readers are left unchanged: their schema guards naturally skip gate_error_v1'
scenarios: A plan-review gate hits an infra exception; a gate_error_v1 REVIEW_RESULT is written; attest.py's _sidecar_branch_decision reads latest_review_timestamp and sees 'just now', making within_window=True even though the last real review was stale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `latest_review_timestamp` (which is schema-agnostic) should treat `gate_error_v1` records — either exclude them from the timestamp scan (add a schema check), or explicitly acknowledge and accept that infra-failure events reset the remediation freshness window.
evidence: plan: 'Written on the existing event types — REVIEW_RESULT (plan/code-review) and COMPLETION_VERDICT (completion) — so no new event type is introduced'
`latest_review_timestamp` filters files by `f.endswith(f'-{EVENT_TYPE}.json')` only — no schema check; it returns the timestamp of the NEWEST matching file regardless of schema
`attest.remediation_mode_candidate` uses `latest_review_timestamp` to gate `within_window`; previously an infra-fail preflight wrote NOTHING to REVIEW_RESULT, so the window was unaffected
plan is entirely silent on this reader and its schema-agnostic timestamp read
findings.finding: `latest_review_timestamp` reads ALL REVIEW_RESULT filenames by suffix (no schema check), and the plan silently resets the remediation-mode freshness window on an infra-failure path that previously emitted no REVIEW_RESULT event. An ERROR sidecar written to REVIEW_RESULT will update the window marker, changing the semantics of 'last review of ANY kind' to include gate errors — a new, unacknowledged side-effect.
findings.location: Files & approach section; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:213-244 (latest_review_timestamp), src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:286,338
scenarios: A plan-review preflight fails (LLMUnavailableError) during a BLOCK remediation loop → gate_error_v1 is written as REVIEW_RESULT → latest_review_timestamp returns this file's timestamp → remediation window resets, making the ticket appear as freshly reviewed when it was not successfully reviewed at all
Conversely, a ticket whose last successful review was 55 minutes ago (outside the window) gets a gate error → window resets to 'just now' → remediation mode is incorrectly granted on the next retry
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) have `latest_review_timestamp` skip files whose parsed payload has `schema == 'gate_error_v1'` (consistent with the schema-guard philosophy for other readers), or (b) add a sentence to the plan explicitly accepting this side-effect as intentional (e.g., 'an infra failure resets the freshness window, preventing remediation mode from applying stale context').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Wrap `emit_error(...)` in a best-effort try/except (log and suppress) on all three gate paths so that a sidecar write failure never alters the gate's pre-existing outcome (INDETERMINATE return or LLMUnavailableError re-raise).
evidence: 'preserving the existing per-gate outcome' — asserted but not enforced if emit_error raises
No try/except or best-effort wrapper around emit_error mentioned in the plan
The sidecar write is file I/O (a new failure point) on the already-exception-handling path
findings.finding: The `emit_error(...)` call is placed inside the existing `except LLMUnavailableError` block with no guard against the sidecar write itself raising an exception, which would silently alter the gate's existing behavior contract: on the plan-review/code-review path a write failure would propagate a new exception instead of returning INDETERMINATE; on the completion path a write failure would raise the wrong exception instead of the original LLMUnavailableError.
findings.location: Files & approach — 'plan-review + code-review infra sites: emit the ERROR record, then `return` the existing degraded INDETERMINATE verdict' and 'completion infra site: wrap preflight (:651) so it emits the ERROR record then re-raises'
scenarios: Sidecar storage is temporarily unavailable (disk full, permission error) — plan-review gate raises instead of returning INDETERMINATE, breaking the soft-degrade contract
Completion gate re-raises a sidecar IOError instead of LLMUnavailableError, confusing upstream callers that catch the specific exception type
findings.suggested_fix:
```python
try:
    emit_error(gate, cause=e, evidence_ref=...)
except Exception as write_err:  # best-effort — never shadow the gate contract
    log.warning('gate_error_sidecar write failed: %s', write_err)
```
Apply this pattern at all three emit_error call sites before the existing `return degraded_verdict` or `raise` statement.

findings.finding: The new ERROR payload needs a schema tag (analogous to `plan_review_result_v2` / `completion_verifier_fail_v1`) but none is named in the plan. Existing readers in both sidecar modules have hard schema guards that skip any unrecognised schema string — meaning an ERROR payload written under an undeclared or novel schema tag will be silently ignored by `latest_review_result` / `latest_fail_verdict`.
findings.location: ## Files — `sidecar.py`, `completion_sidecar.py` — 'add an optional ERROR verdict + error object to the payload builders'

findings.finding: Both `completion_sidecar.build_payload` and `plan_review/sidecar.build_payload` route through `reconcile_verdict` (completion side) or assume `verdict` is already a PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE string. `reconcile_verdict` normalises any non-PASS verdict to FAIL, which would overwrite `verdict='ERROR'` before it reaches the persisted payload. The plan says 'add an optional ERROR verdict … to the payload builders' but does not specify whether the ERROR path bypasses this guardrail.
findings.location: ## Files — `completion_sidecar.py` — `build_payload` / `reconcile_verdict` interaction
findings.suggested_fix: Add an early-return branch at the top of `build_payload`: if the incoming verdict dict already carries `verdict='ERROR'` (or the builder is called via a dedicated `build_error_payload` helper), emit the ERROR schema directly without calling `reconcile_verdict`.

findings.finding: The schema-guarded readers in plan_review/sidecar.py (latest_review_result, all_review_results) only accept schema values 'plan_review_result_v1'/'plan_review_result_v2'; an ERROR record written to REVIEW_RESULT with a distinct or unspecified schema will be silently skipped by these readers. The plan does not specify the schema tag for the ERROR payload, nor enumerate these readers as consumers that may need updating.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` — add optional ERROR verdict + error object to payload builders.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) introduce a distinct schema tag (e.g. `plan_review_error_v1`) and confirm the existing schema guards correctly skip it (readers see no ERROR rows — intentional), or (b) add that tag to the guard list and define what the ERROR payload looks like when returned. Document the choice explicitly in the Approach section.

findings.finding: The plan adds the ERROR outcome to completion_sidecar.py's payload builder, but does not enumerate latest_fail_verdict() and latest_pass_record() — both of which are schema-guarded to 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'/'completion_verifier_pass_v1' — as consumers to evaluate. An ERROR record under COMPLETION_VERDICT with a different or unspecified schema will be silently skipped by both readers.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` — add optional ERROR verdict + error object to payload builders.

findings.finding: The schema tag for the ERROR sidecar record is unspecified. Both sidecar modules apply schema-string guards on reads (`'plan_review_result_v2'` in `latest_review_result`; `SCHEMA`/`SCHEMA_PASS` in `latest_fail_verdict`/`latest_pass_record`). If the ERROR record reuses an existing schema tag, existing readers will return it unexpectedly (callers never expect `verdict='ERROR'`); if it uses a new schema tag, existing readers will silently skip it — neither outcome is specified and no reader update is mentioned.
findings.location: Files section / sidecar.py, completion_sidecar.py; Approach section / 'no new event type is introduced'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a distinct schema tag (e.g. `plan_review_result_error_v1`) and update the schema guards in both readers to explicitly skip it, so they continue returning only PASS/BLOCK/FAIL records while the ERROR record is separately queryable by a new reader or direct jq.

findings.finding: The plan says 'write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising,' but on the plan-review gate (and code-review gate), infra exceptions (LLMUnavailableError) are currently caught and converted to an INDETERMINATE verdict — they are NOT re-raised. Implementing 'write sidecar then re-raise' on those paths would break the existing fail-soft contract (replacing a graceful INDETERMINATE degrade with a hard propagated exception). Only the completion gate re-raises from preflight. The plan does not reconcile this asymmetry.
findings.location: Approach section / Files: gate_dispatch.py
findings.suggested_fix: Split the approach into two cases: (a) gates that already degrade (plan-review, code-review): write ERROR sidecar in the catch block before calling _degraded_*_verdict — still returns INDETERMINATE, no re-raise; (b) completion gate which already re-raises: write ERROR sidecar immediately before the raise (in produce_completion_verdict's mid-run failure branch and preflight path). Update the Tests section to exercise both behaviors.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that gate_dispatch will 'write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising' on the infra-exception path, but the existing infra-exception handling in gate_dispatch does NOT re-raise: `produce_plan_review_verdict`'s two `except LLMUnavailableError` blocks both `return _degraded_plan_review_verdict(...)` (a soft degrade); only `produce_completion_verdict` propagates. Gating ERROR+re-raise against the current soft-degrade design is an unverified structural change claim that would require one of the two paths to be rearchitected.
findings.location: ## Files — gate_dispatch.py bullet; ## Tests AC1

findings.finding: The plan's core mechanism — 'write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising' — does not match the actual plan-review and code-review gate control flow, where infra exceptions are caught and RETURNED as INDETERMINATE verdicts, not re-raised. Only the completion gate propagates the exception. The implementer following the plan's 'before re-raising' framing will look for a try/except…raise site that does not exist on the plan-review path, leading to either a misplaced write or a missed write.
findings.location: ## Approach / gate_dispatch.py lines 138-143, 177-180 (plan-review catches LLMUnavailableError and returns INDETERMINATE, never re-raises); lines 652-682 (completion propagates)
findings.suggested_fix: For plan-review: write the ERROR sidecar at the top of `_degraded_plan_review_verdict` (before partitioning/returning), passing `ctx.ticket_id` as the ticket anchor. For code-review: write inside `_degraded_code_review_verdict` when `request.target_ticket` is non-None (no ticket, no sidecar). For completion: write before the `raise LLMError(...)` at line 679, using `ticket_id`. The plan should describe these three distinct insertion points rather than a single 'before re-raising' framing.

findings.finding: Readers of the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar — specifically `latest_review_result`, `all_review_results`, `attest.py` (remediation-mode eligibility), and `plan_review/__init__.py` (novelty prior-findings) — are not enumerated as consumers that the plan's ERROR sidecar write could affect. If an ERROR record is emitted as a `REVIEW_RESULT` event with the same schema tag (`plan_review_result_v2`), `latest_review_result` will return it as the newest valid payload, injecting `verdict='ERROR'` into the remediation-eligibility check (`attest.py`) and the novelty prior-findings set (`plan_review/__init__.py`). The plan does not address how these readers should handle or ignore ERROR payloads.
findings.location: Files section / Approach section — plan_review/sidecar.py
findings.suggested_fix: Use a distinct schema tag (e.g. `plan_review_error_v1`) for ERROR payloads so the schema guards in `latest_review_result` and `latest_fail_verdict` skip them by construction — no reader changes required, and the ERROR record is still persisted and compaction-preserved.

findings.finding: The proposed `error.cause` sub-object on the ERROR sidecar payload partially rebuilds existing classified-failure machinery: `failure.py` already exposes `outcome_of(error)` → `resolution_fields(outcome)` producing `{resolution_class, retryable, diagnostic}` (sanitized, allowlisted), and the degraded-verdict paths already write `coverage.llm_error = str(error)` and `**resolution_fields(outcome)`. The plan does not explain why a parallel `error{cause, ...}` struct is needed rather than reusing this existing pipeline.
findings.location: ## Approach / Files: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py, src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py
findings.suggested_fix: In `build_payload` for the ERROR branch, derive `error.cause` from `failure.outcome_of(exc).diagnostic` (already allowlisted + redacted) rather than from `str(exc)` directly, aligning with the existing sanitized-diagnostic pattern.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
